Começar sessão

Tutor: Gui, sessão 8 — direto ao ponto.

Imagina que você perdeu o celular com o app da sua corretora. Não tem senha salva, não tem nada. Mas os seus Bitcoin continuam lá, intocados.

Como isso é possível se o dispositivo sumiu?

Student: Ué Comp, se eles não tem minha private key eles não conseguem acessar a carteira.

Tutor: Boa intuição. Mas me faz pensar numa coisa — onde estão esses Bitcoin, afinal? No celular, ou em outro lugar?

Student: Ahh, óbvio que eles não estão no celular. Continuam na rede e precisam da minha private key para autenticar uma transação.

Tutor: Exato. Então o que é uma carteira de criptomoedas, de verdade? O que ela guarda?

Student: A private key?

Tutor: Isso. E se a carteira guarda a private key, o que acontece se alguém rouba a carteira — ou se você perde o acesso a ela?

Student: Bom tem outros níveis de segurança até conseguir acessar a private key da carteira (igual a metamask que pede uma senha), mas de forma simplificada se alguém rouba ela eu perco o acesso a tudo. Não tem uma organização centralizada para me dar acesso de novo.

Tutor: Perfeito — você tocou num ponto crítico: sem organização centralizada, não tem "esqueci minha senha". 

Agora, existe um mecanismo que permite você recuperar o acesso à sua carteira mesmo perdendo o dispositivo. Você já ouviu falar em seed phrase?

Student: Sim. Mas no caso ela é o mesmo que a private key de certa forma por que o hash dela resulta na pk.

Tutor: Quase. Mas pensa assim — uma seed phrase pode gerar múltiplas private keys, uma pra cada criptomoeda ou conta diferente. Se fosse a mesma coisa, como você explicaria isso?

Student: Uhmmm ai você me pegou ein. Não sei, algorítmos de hash diferente. Ou o hash é seed + algum identificador do blockchain.

Tutor: Boa intuição na segunda hipótese — é mais ou menos isso. A seed é a raiz, e a partir dela um algoritmo determinístico deriva múltiplas chaves usando índices diferentes (caminho de derivação). 

Isso tem um nome: carteira HD — Hierarchical Deterministic. Mas o conceito central você já sacou.

Agora, dado tudo isso — qual é a diferença prática entre uma carteira custodial, tipo a da Binance, e uma não-custodial, tipo a MetaMask?

Student: Não sei o que significa custodial nesse sentido.

Tutor: Pensa na Binance: quando você deposita lá, quem guarda a private key — você ou a Binance?

Student: Ahh a binance. Eles abstraem essa parte.

Tutor: Exato. E aí o que isso significa na prática pra você como usuário — quais são os riscos?

Student: Que pode leak. Eu ouvi falar de algumas corretoras (ou carteiras custodiais pelo jeito) que tiveram vazamentos enormes.

Tutor: Isso, e tem um risco ainda mais fundamental — se a Binance falir ou bloquear sua conta, o que acontece com seus fundos?

Student: Fico sem eles. Caraca Comp não tinha pensado nisso.

Tutor: Exato — e isso é o que o pessoal de cripto resume em "not your keys, not your coins."

Então recapitulando o que você construiu: carteira guarda a private key, não os fundos; seed phrase é a raiz que deriva múltiplas chaves; custodial significa terceiro guarda sua chave, com todos os riscos que isso implica.

Pergunta de aplicação: você está construindo um dApp. Faz mais sentido integrar com uma carteira custodial ou não-custodial? Por quê?

Student: Bom cada um deve ter seus prós e contras, mas considerando que ainda não estou pensando em nenhum pró para o custodial além do fato de ser mais abstraído e simples, eu preferiria ter em uma não custodial. Ai a responsabilidade de segurança é minha e não de outra pessoa

Tutor: Boa. E tem um ponto técnico aí além da segurança — num dApp, se o usuário usa carteira não-custodial, quem assina as transações é ele, direto, sem intermediário. Isso é o que garante o "trustless" que você já conhece de blockchain.

Custodial quebra isso — você estaria confiando na corretora pra assinar por você.

Bora pro próximo conceito. Você mencionou lá atrás que ficou confuso sobre por que um smart contract precisa rodar em múltiplos nós — por que não um servidor central só. Ainda tá confuso nisso?

Student: Não. Precisa rodar em múltiplos nós pois precisamos fazer várias verificações para certificar-se de que o resultado final é o mesmo.

Tutor: Certo. E aí entra uma pergunta mais difícil — se cada nó roda o mesmo contrato de forma independente, como todos eles garantem que estão executando *exatamente* o mesmo código, na mesma versão, produzindo o mesmo resultado? O que impede um nó com hardware diferente de chegar num resultado diferente?

Student: Hahaha vou usar a resposta de sempre e falar que tem haver com usar um hash e comparar todos eles para certificar-se de que são o mesmo

Tutor: Haha — o hash é sua resposta pra tudo, e honestamente não tá errado como verificação de integridade do código. Mas o problema que eu tô levantando é diferente.

Imagina: mesmo código, mas um nó roda em Windows, outro em Linux, outro num chip ARM. O resultado de uma operação de ponto flutuante pode ser diferente dependendo do hardware.

Como você resolveria isso?

Student: Uhmm ata, não tinha entendido o escopo da pergunta. A gente padronzia o ambiente ao usar um ambiente virtual.

Tutor: Exatamente — e esse ambiente virtual é a EVM, a Ethereum Virtual Machine.

Todo nó da rede roda a EVM, que é uma máquina virtual com comportamento 100% determinístico e independente de hardware. Mesmo contrato, qualquer máquina, resultado idêntico.

Agora — rodar código em milhares de nós simultaneamente tem um custo. Como você acha que o Ethereum resolve o problema de alguém mandar um loop infinito pra travar a rede inteira?

Student: Cobra para rodar o código ou interagir de qualquer forma permanente com os smart contracts na EVM.

Tutor: Isso — e esse custo tem nome: gas. Cada operação consome uma quantidade de gas, e se o gas acabar antes do código terminar, a execução para. Loop infinito resolvido.

Bora pro próximo. Você já mexeu com mappings em Solidity?

Student: Só queria tocar em um ponto. Isso é engraçado que você falou de o gas acabar por que nas minhas interações com a metamask ele sempre me avisa quanto vai custar antes de eu confirmar a transação.

Tutor: Exato — a MetaMask está estimando o gas necessário antes de você confirmar. E se a transação falhar no meio por falta de gas, o gas já consumido não volta. Você paga pelo trabalho computacional feito, mesmo que o resultado não seja o que você queria.

Agora, mappings — já mexeu?

Student: Ss

Tutor: Como você descreveria o que um mapping faz em Solidity?

Student: O mapping faz uma estratégia como dicionário.

Tutor: Isso. E como você declararia um mapping que associa um endereço de carteira ao saldo de tokens desse endereço?

Student: Ai você me pegou, não me lembro da sintaxe. mappings(int 16 -> alguma coisa) var?

Tutor: Tudo bem, vou te mostrar e a gente trabalha em cima disso.

```solidity
mapping(address => uint256) public saldos;
```

Três partes: o tipo da chave (`address`), o tipo do valor (`uint256`), e o nome da variável (`saldos`).

Como você leria o saldo de um endereço específico a partir desse mapping?

Student: Também não sei Comp. E nem entendi direito a sintaxe. Address já foi declarado como um novo tipo de dado através de algum tipo de struct? Não entendi direito, você poderia dar um exemplo?

## PHASE 1 — EVIDENCE ANALYSIS

<concept_analysis id="crypto_wallets">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (early):** Without any scaffolding, the student immediately identified the key insight — "se eles não tem minha private key eles não conseguem acessar a carteira" — correctly framing the private key as the access mechanism, not the device. This was the opening exchange; no hints had been given.

- **Level 5 (early):** On follow-up, the student independently clarified that Bitcoin live on the network and require the private key to authorize a transaction — "Continuam na rede e precisam da minha private key para autenticar uma transação." This was reasoning, not recall.

- **Level 5 (early-to-mid):** When asked what happens if the wallet is stolen, the student independently introduced a nuance the Companion had not introduced: multi-layer security (MetaMask password) before private key access, then correctly identified the catastrophic risk — "se alguém rouba ela eu perco o acesso a tudo. Não tem uma organização centralizada para me dar acesso de novo."

- **Level 4 (mid):** On seed phrase vs. private key, the student made a partially incorrect claim — "ela é o mesmo que a private key de certa forma por que o hash dela resulta na pk." The Companion challenged this (one seed generates multiple keys), and the student hit an impasse ("Uhmmm ai você me pegou ein"), then self-generated a plausible hypothesis: "O hash é seed + algum identificador do blockchain" — which the Companion confirmed was directionally correct. The student did not fully resolve the misconception (HD wallet derivation) but reasoned toward the right model independently after the impasse.

- **Level 2 (mid):** The student did not know what "custodial" meant — "Não sei o que significa custodial nesse sentido." The Companion narrowed to a concrete example (Binance holds the key). The student then answered correctly ("Eles abstraem essa parte").

- **Level 5 (mid):** After understanding custodial, the student independently extended to the risk: "Pode leak. Eu ouvi falar de algumas corretoras que tiveram vazamentos enormes." This was beyond what the Companion had asked.

- **Level 5 (late):** When asked about the dApp integration choice, the student reasoned through the trade-off independently and chose non-custodial, with substantive reasoning: "a responsabilidade de segurança é minha e não de outra pessoa." Notably, the student acknowledged considering custodial pros but found none beyond simplicity.

**Trajectory:** Strongly improving. Opened at Level 5 (independent correct reasoning), maintained that level through most of the discussion, with one Level 4 self-correction at an impasse and one Level 2 dip when vocabulary was unfamiliar. No decline was observed.

**Misconceptions:** The seed-as-private-key equivalence was a partially incorrect model — the student said "o hash dela resulta na pk" (conflating one-to-one with one-to-many derivation). After scaffolding, the student generated the correct direction ("seed + algum identificador") and accepted the HD wallet explanation. This was not fully articulated in the student's own words as a resolved misconception — the Companion provided the resolution framing. Classify as **weak partially-resolved misconception**: the student moved toward the right model but did not verbalize the correction independently.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion is strongly satisfied across most of the discussion — the student arrived with prior knowledge and produced multiple Level 5 moments without scaffolding, including one independent risk extension (data leak from custodial) that went beyond the question. The Level 4 self-correction on seed phrase adds weight. The one persisting-adjacent weakness (seed = key conflation not fully verbalized as resolved) is weak and does not substantially constrain the score. Trajectory criterion supports a higher score. This is a first-exposure concept, but the student's prior asymmetric cryptography knowledge served as a strong activated prerequisite.

</concept_analysis>

<concept_analysis id="evm_and_virtualization">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (early):** Asked why smart contracts run on multiple nodes, the student answered independently and correctly: "Precisamos fazer várias verificações para certificar-se de que o resultado final é o mesmo." This directly resolved the self-reported confusion from the prior session (ethereum_platform profile notes this as the main confusion point). This was unprompted and substantively reasoned, not recalled.

- **Level 5 (mid):** When asked about the hardware-heterogeneity problem (Windows vs. Linux vs. ARM producing different floating-point results), the student initially misunderstood the scope ("vou usar a resposta de sempre e falar que tem haver com usar um hash"). After the Companion clarified the scope, the student correctly identified the solution independently: "A gente padroniza o ambiente ao usar um ambiente virtual." This was stated without further scaffolding once the problem was properly framed — it required one scope-clarification from the Companion, making it borderline Level 2/5. The Companion's clarification reframed the problem but did not give the answer, so classifying as **Level 5** after appropriate scope-setting.

- **Level 5 (mid):** On the gas/loop-infinite problem, the student answered before the Companion completed the explanation: "Cobra para rodar o código ou interagir de qualquer forma permanente com os smart contracts na EVM." This was a clean independent correct response.

- **Level 6 (late):** The student independently connected gas to their personal MetaMask experience — "nas minhas interações com a metamask ele sempre me avisa quanto vai custar antes de eu confirmar a transação" — making an unsolicited connection between the abstract gas concept and its concrete manifestation in a tool they use. This was not prompted; it was the student extending the discussion themselves.

**Trajectory:** Improving. The distributed-execution rationale (previously the stated confusion point) was resolved independently at the session's opening. Subsequent questions were answered at Level 5 or Level 6 throughout, with no scaffolding dependency after the initial scope clarification.

**Misconceptions:** None identified. The hash-as-answer attempt was a misread of the question's scope, not an incorrect mental model about the EVM.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion is strongly satisfied — the student resolved a flagged prior confusion point unprompted, correctly identified virtual machine standardization independently, correctly identified the gas mechanism independently, and generated a novel personal connection (MetaMask experience) without prompting. The trajectory criterion is positive. This is first exposure, but the student performed at Level 5–6 across multiple exchanges. Calibration anchors note that scoring above 0.50 on first exposure requires Level 5 or 6 evidence across multiple exchanges — that threshold is met here with room.

</concept_analysis>

<concept_analysis id="solidity_mappings">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 1 (early):** "Ss" — passive acknowledgment when asked if they had worked with mappings. Followed by a surface-level definition: "O mapping faz uma estratégia como dicionário." Directionally correct but minimal.

- **Level 2 (early):** When asked to declare a mapping for address-to-balance, the student could not recall the syntax — "não me lembro da sintaxe. mappings(int 16 -> alguma coisa) var?" — wrong type, wrong arrow direction syntax, wrong structure. Required the Companion to provide the correct declaration.

- **Level 2 (mid):** After seeing the declaration, student asked what `address` was — "Address já foi declarado como um novo tipo de dado através de algum tipo de struct?" — suggesting no prior independent understanding of the Solidity type system. Required explanation.

- **Level 2 (mid):** After the Companion showed the read/write syntax, the student asked about `uint256`: "Tá então uint declara o tipo de valor que vai ser guardado em saldos[n]?" — correct interpretation but derived from the Companion's explanation, not independent.

- **Level 3→5 (mid):** The student wrote the `adicionarTokens` function with the correct logic and correctly identified `self.address` as the concept (wrong Solidity syntax, right idea). After being told the Solidity term (`msg.sender`), the student then independently identified the security problem: "Sim, seria horrível qualquer pessoa poder adicionar saldos infinitamente." This was the student generating the security concern without being asked to identify a security problem — the Companion asked if they could identify a security problem, and the student did so correctly and concisely.

- **Level 2 (mid):** When pushed to resolve the access control problem, the student could not reason through where to store the owner address — "Eu não estou conseguindo entender exatamente o problema" — and required full scaffolding including the `constructor` and `require` pattern.

- **Level 5 (mid-to-late):** In the voting contract exercise, the student independently wrote both mappings correctly — `mapping(address => bool) public ja_votou` and the general structure for vote counts — and independently included the `require` with the correct condition and the double-mapping update logic. This was the student applying the concept without scaffolding on a new problem.

- **Level 2 (late):** Two syntax errors in the voting exercise required correction: `False` vs. `false`, and using `msg.sender` as the key for `num_votos` instead of `candidato`. These were syntax/logic issues, not conceptual gaps about mappings per se. The second error (confusing the subject of the mapping key) may reflect a mild conceptual gap about which entity is the index.

- **Level 5 (late):** After the corrections, the student produced the final correct version independently.

**Trajectory:** Mixed but improving. Early responses showed no independent syntactic knowledge and required the Companion to provide the full declaration. By mid-session, the student could apply the concept to a new scenario with partial errors. The final exercise was mostly correct with minor errors corrected after single exchanges. Evidence level improved from Level 2 (early) to Level 5 (late) with one self-generated function attempt along the way.

**Misconceptions:** No structured misconceptions identified. The `False` vs. `false` capitalization and the `msg.sender` vs. `candidato` key confusion are syntax errors and a minor logical slip, not incorrect mental models about how mappings work. The `self` vs. `msg.sender` confusion reflects Python/other-language background, not a Solidity-specific misconception.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion was partially satisfied by mid-to-late session — the student independently applied the mapping pattern to a new voting problem with moderate success. The trajectory was improving but the concept was first exposure and required significant scaffolding early (Level 2 throughout the initial example). The Level 5 performance on the independent voting exercise carries moderate weight but is tempered by the two errors in the final code. This fits the "first exposure with scaffolded → independent" pattern warranting moderate weight from the trajectory criterion.

</concept_analysis>

<concept_analysis id="smart_contract_security">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (early):** When asked what could go wrong with a withdraw-then-zero-balance pattern, the student independently identified the core vulnerability: "e se a função parasse de executar antes de zerar o saldo? Ai ele conseguiria continuar enviando dinheiro infinitamente." This was independent, correctly reasoned, and arrived at the reentrancy concept before it was named.

- **Level 2 (mid):** When asked how an attacker would force the function to stop mid-execution, the student could not reason through the mechanism — "Ai eu já não sei. Por que não está rodando em vários computadores?" — an incorrect framing that required the Companion to redirect.

- **Level 3→5 (mid):** After the Companion reframed (a malicious contract can execute code when receiving ETH), the student worked through the recursive-call mechanism independently: "o contrato 1 envia para o contrato 2 e antes de enviar uma mensagem de confirmado para que o contrato 1 possa continuar executando todo código, o contrato 2 faz outro pedido de sacar?" — this is substantively correct and was the student's own formulation of the reentrancy flow.

- **Level 2 (mid):** When asked about a protection mechanism beyond ordering, the student suggested "não deixar qualquer conta interagir com o contrato" — directionally wrong (would break all users), required Companion to redirect.

- **Level 3 (mid):** After a leading hint ("como você impediria que a função fosse chamada enquanto ela já está sendo executada?"), the student correctly proposed the mutex pattern: "Criar um if executing then error." This is the reentrancy guard concept. The student arrived at the right answer after a narrowing question, so this is Level 3 rather than Level 5.

- **Level 5 (late):** At the closing of the session, the student independently explained reentrancy in their own words: "Quando um mal feitor tenta se aproveitar de um contrato ao chamar ele por outro contrato e usar a não finalização dele como método para chamar funções antes delas serem finalizadas." This was unprompted, self-generated language that captured the mechanism correctly.

**Trajectory:** Improving. The student opened with Level 5 (independent identification of the vulnerability pattern), dipped to Level 2 on the mechanism details, recovered to Level 3-5 on the call-flow formulation, and ended with Level 5 independent synthesis. The mid-session dip on the specific mechanism required scaffolding, but the student recovered and demonstrated genuine understanding of the core concept by the end.

**Misconceptions:** The distributed-nodes framing ("por que não está rodando em vários computadores?") was an incorrect mental model of the problem scope — the student attributed the reentrancy vulnerability to network architecture rather than contract execution flow. This was corrected by the Companion, and the student subsequently reasoned correctly about the call flow. **Resolved misconception** — the student's own formulation of the reentrancy mechanism afterward ("o contrato 2 faz outro pedido de sacar") correctly locates the problem in execution flow, not network architecture. However, the student did not verbalize why the distributed-nodes framing was wrong.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion: the student independently identified the core vulnerability (Level 5) and produced a correct self-generated synthesis at closing (Level 5). The mid-session mechanism required scaffolding (Level 2–3), which tempers the score. The resolved misconception about network architecture is not a strong cap signal — it was corrected and the subsequent formulation was correct. The trajectory improving criterion applies, with stronger independent performance at the beginning and end. First exposure with Level 5 bookends and Level 2–3 in the middle.

</concept_analysis>

<concept_analysis id="software_testing_and_quality">

Evidence moments:

- **Level 5 (mid):** When asked what to verify first in a test of `votar()`, the student independently identified the two test priorities in the correct order: "Se o require funciona. Depois se a variável realmente foi atualizada." This maps directly to negative-path and positive-path testing without being told the categories.

- **Level 3 (mid):** When asked to describe the test for double-voting step by step, the student correctly identified the three steps (call `votar()`, call again, expect revert), but admitted not knowing how to do the setup — "Eu não faço ideia de como fazer o setup (criar um contrato na memória para testar)." This was scaffolded performance in the sense that the Companion then showed the full test structure, but the student's prior step identification was independent.

- **Level 1 (mid-late):** After seeing the `vm.expectRevert()` example, the student gave `>>>` twice — no engagement — and then claimed the "overrule" test was a joke and said "O fluxo faz sentido." This is passive acknowledgment — Level 1.

- **Level 2 (late):** When asked to write the positive test, the student attempted it and got most of the logic right but made two errors: not prefixing the function with `test_`, and accessing `num_votos` as a local variable rather than `votacao.num_votos(1)`. After the Companion provided the correct version, the student acknowledged.

- **Level 5 (late):** Before attempting the test, the student asked a substantive clarifying question: "De onde vem o 'votacao' o 'vm'? O que está acontecendo nesse teste?" — this is genuine conceptual confusion, not passive acknowledgment, and demonstrates that the student was processing the structure rather than accepting it. After the explanation, the student correctly articulated the two components: "`votacao.votar(1)` chama a função no contrato instanciado, e `vm.expectRevert()` diz pro Foundry 'a próxima chamada deve falhar.'" This was an accurate restatement in the student's own language — borderline Level 3 (needed explanation, then reformulated independently).

**Trajectory:** Mixed. The student showed strong conceptual intuition early (Level 5 on test priority ordering) but could not independently write the test setup or access the mapping correctly. The final exercise had two errors. Trajectory is roughly flat across the section — conceptual understanding is present but procedural execution is limited.

**Misconceptions:** No structural misconceptions. The lack of Foundry/testing knowledge is a genuine knowledge gap (student explicitly said all their test experience came from AI assistance), not an incorrect mental model.

**Assessment rationale:** Independence criterion: the student demonstrated Level 5 understanding of test structure (negative vs. positive path, step-by-step logic) but Level 2 on procedural execution. The student's admission that all prior testing was AI-generated is relevant — this is genuinely first exposure at the procedural level. The clarifying question about `votacao` and `vm` was substantive and led to a correct reformulation, which adds moderate evidence. Trajectory was flat. Calibration anchors: first exposure with some Level 5 conceptual but Level 2 procedural — score should reflect partial but not deep understanding.

</concept_analysis>

symmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Can independently articulate the symmetric model (shared key, both parties must possess it) and correctly identifies key distribution as its fundamental weakness. Applied this knowledge to reframe a novel 'two private keys' proposal as equivalent to the symmetric problem. Coverage was limited to the core concept — mechanics like block vs. stream ciphers, key sizes, or specific algorithms were not explored. Next session should probe whether the student can describe a concrete scenario where symmetric cryptography is appropriate and why, to verify understanding extends beyond the weakness framing.

asymmetric_cryptography.evidence_summary: Early misconception (sender uses private key to encrypt for confidentiality, rather than recipient's public key) was partially resolved — the student demonstrated correct directional usage in subsequent exchanges but did not verbally articulate why the earlier reasoning was wrong. Later evidence was Level 5 (digital signing as intentional directional inversion) and Level 6 (combined asymmetric + hash solution). Trajectory improved strongly after the early misconception.
asymmetric_cryptography.source_notes: Understands the two-directional logic of asymmetric cryptography and correctly distinguishes encryption (recipient's public key encrypts, recipient's private key decrypts) from signing (sender's private key signs, sender's public key verifies) as inverse operations. The initial key-direction confusion was corrected in behavior but not explicitly verbalized — the student said 'meu exemplo faz bastante sentido' even after being corrected, suggesting partial residual resistance. Next session should open by asking the student to walk through both directions (encrypt for confidentiality, sign for authenticity) unprompted to verify the distinction is now stable and accessible.

digital_signatures.evidence_summary: Session ended after two student turns — the only substantive response ('única e não fungível') was vague analogical language that required immediate redirection (Level 2 at best), and the closing diagnostic question about which key to use for signing went unanswered (Level 0). No independent reasoning about the cryptographic mechanism was observed.
digital_signatures.source_notes: Concept was introduced via physical signature analogy but the session ended before the student engaged with the cryptographic mechanism at all. The 'não fungível' response suggests loose borrowing from blockchain vocabulary rather than reasoning about digital signatures. Next session must resume from the unanswered question: ask the student which key (public or private) they would use to sign a document and why, before progressing to the hash-then-sign structure. The student's prior working knowledge of asymmetric key directions (from the asymmetric_cryptography profile) may activate quickly once the question is posed — the prerequisite knowledge is there but the connection to signing has not been made yet.

evm_and_virtualization.evidence_summary: Level 5 independent performance across three distinct aspects — distributed execution rationale, virtual machine as hardware standardization, and gas as execution cost — with one Level 6 moment (student independently connected gas estimation to their personal MetaMask experience without prompting). Trajectory was consistently strong, with the prior session's flagged confusion point (why distributed execution is necessary) resolved independently at the session's opening.
evm_and_virtualization.source_notes: Independently resolved the distributed-execution rationale that was flagged as a confusion point in the prior session, correctly identified virtual machine standardization as the cross-platform determinism solution, and connected gas to the MetaMask cost-estimation UI from personal experience. Concept was first exposure but performed at Level 5–6 across multiple exchanges. Next session should probe whether the student can explain the EVM's role without prompting and articulate why determinism is essential for consensus — the gas-incentive structure and its connection to miner economics has not yet been explored.

smart_contract_security.evidence_summary: Opened with Level 5 independent identification of the reentrancy vulnerability pattern (state not zeroed before funds sent allows infinite drain) and closed with Level 5 self-generated explanation of the attack in plain language. Mid-session mechanism details required scaffolding (Level 2–3): the student initially misattributed the vulnerability to distributed network architecture rather than execution flow, which was corrected by the Companion. The reentrancy guard (mutex pattern) was reached after one narrowing question (Level 3).
smart_contract_security.source_notes: Independently identified the core reentrancy vulnerability and produced a correct plain-language explanation at the session's close. The mechanism — contract-to-contract recursive callback before state update — required scaffolding to reach but was subsequently articulated in the student's own words. The Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern was named by the Companion, not the student. Next session should present a new vulnerable contract snippet and ask the student to identify the vulnerability and propose the fix without scaffolding, to test whether the pattern has been internalized rather than just recognized in the explained example.

software_testing_and_quality.evidence_summary: Level 5 conceptual performance on test structure (independently identified negative-path and positive-path testing priorities in correct order without the category names) but Level 2 procedural performance (could not write test setup, made two structural errors in the attempted function). Student explicitly disclosed that all prior testing was AI-generated, confirming genuine first exposure at the implementation level. Trajectory was flat.
software_testing_and_quality.source_notes: Grasps the conceptual logic of test design — correctly identified that a voting test should first verify rejection of invalid inputs, then verify state updates — but cannot independently write a Foundry test: missed the `test_` naming convention, accessed the mapping directly instead of through the contract instance, and did not know how to instantiate a contract for testing. All prior testing experience was AI-assisted. Next session should ask the student to write a complete test from scratch for a simple contract (e.g., the `adicionarTokens` function from this session) without showing an example first, to establish a baseline for procedural execution.

computer_networks_fundamentals.evidence_summary: Uneven coverage: early DNS understanding was scaffolded and incomplete (centralized framing required correction; DNS hierarchy was accepted but not reformulated independently), while packet-switching rationale and routing propagation were explained independently and correctly at Level 5. Student explicitly self-identified a knowledge gap in protocol layers (OSI model). Trajectory was flat-to-improving, with stronger independent performance in the second half of the section.
computer_networks_fundamentals.source_notes: Can independently reason about why packet-switching exists (resilience and parallelism) and how routing tables propagate through neighbor queries, but does not have an independent model of DNS resolution or the TCP/IP stack. The centralized-DNS starting assumption suggests incomplete mental model of how distributed naming works. Next session should open by asking the student to explain what happens between typing a URL and receiving a response — without any prompting — to assess which parts of the flow are independently retrievable.

p2p_networks.evidence_summary: Consistently strong Level 5 and Level 6 evidence throughout the section. Student independently reconstructed BitTorrent's core mechanism from earlier DHT content, contributed an independent insight about availability-driven latency (not prompted), and produced a closing synthesis that extended the Companion's 'resilience' framing into a four-part failure-mode taxonomy. Trajectory was uniformly strong, benefiting from cumulative context built during the DHT discussion.
p2p_networks.source_notes: Owns the P2P value proposition — can independently articulate why decentralized file distribution outperforms central servers and connect it to blockchain's infrastructure requirements. Generated a four-part resilience taxonomy (malicious actors, bad actors, node departure, node arrival) independently. A loose Tor-P2P association was noted but is not a structural misconception — it reflects incomplete boundary-drawing between network types rather than an incorrect model. Next session should distinguish P2P from client-server and from Tor/overlay networks to sharpen the student's mental model of when each architecture applies.
